Question 1 of 3

What will BUILT sell next quarter?

The most important number in FP&A is the one that doesn't exist yet. Our model produces retailer-level demand forecasts — with a floor, a plan, and a ceiling — 13 weeks forward, updated every SPINS cycle. Each panel below is a live LightGBM prediction for a specific retailer account, not a spreadsheet extrapolation.

Validation: Across 5 rolling quarters (2025–Q1 2026), the model averaged 13.1% wMAPE vs. 35% for a moving-average Excel baseline — a 21.9pp improvement. In the best quarter (Q1 2026, 13-week OOS), it hit 4.4% wMAPE. At Brian's own "$1M per 1pp" anchor, the rolling improvement alone is worth $22M / year.
FP&A payoff: Supply chain gets three numbers — not one. Floor drives safety stock. Ceiling drives capacity reservation. Center drives the operating plan. No more single-point forecasts that blow up when a retailer runs a promotion.
Question 2 of 3

What's actually driving those numbers?

A forecast without a cause is just a guess with confidence intervals. Mo decomposes every demand signal into its drivers — distribution momentum, velocity trends, and causal price effects — so the FP&A team can distinguish earned growth from promotional spend.

Distribution Is the Story at Walmart

For BUILT's top SKUs at Walmart, TDP (Total Distribution Points — the number of stores carrying the product) has been the primary growth driver — not price cuts, not seasonality. The chart below shows the decomposition: green line is TDP trajectory, purple is velocity (sell-through rate per store). When TDP rises but velocity is flat, growth is a distribution story — and Mo knows to weight TDP features heavily in those series.

When Price Moves Matter — The Kroger Case

At Kroger in late 2025, Brownie Batter 4pk dropped from $10.99 → $10.14 (−7.7%). Mo's causal model (BSTS counterfactual) isolates the price effect from everything else happening that week — seasonality, distribution, promo — and answers: how many units were above the counterfactual baseline?

Per-retailer price sensitivity (confirmed negative elasticity):
Retailer Elasticity (ε) Interpretation Planning implication
Walmart −0.245 Inelastic — modest price sensitivity Small price moves have limited volume risk
Kroger −0.590 Moderate — price moves are felt Promo ROI calculations should use ε = −0.59
Ahold Delhaize −1.262 Elastic — demand responds strongly Price changes require demand offset planning
FP&A payoff: Every trade promotion plan now has a causal baseline. "We spent $X on this price cut — Mo shows us it drove $Y in incremental units, net of everything else." That's the ROI calculation trade teams have never had before.
Question 3 of 3

How does Mo explain every forecast to the team?

The question FP&A always asks about a model: "Why did it forecast that number?" Mo answers this in two ways — visually via SHAP driver charts, and conversationally via Mo Chat. Every forecast has an audit trail, in business language, at any level of the hierarchy.

Every Number Has a Business Reason

The SHAP waterfall below shows exactly what moved the Brownie Batter 4pk forecast at Walmart. Each bar is a business factor. The model isn't guessing — it's weighing the same factors a skilled analyst would, but across 2,496 series simultaneously.

Mo Supercharges Your FP&A Team

Mo is not a dashboard. It's an analytics co-pilot that your FP&A and commercial teams can query in plain English — and get answers grounded in live SPINS data, causal models, and the same ML pipeline that drives the forecast.

📊

Forecast Drawer

Per-SKU, per-retailer floor/plan/ceiling for the next 13 weeks. Updated every SPINS cycle. Drill from portfolio → retailer → SKU in three clicks.

💬

Mo Chat

Ask in plain English: "Why is the Walmart forecast up 18% this quarter?" Mo responds with driver decomposition, elasticity context, and a recommended action — grounded in live data.

⚡

Price Scenario Modeling

Slide the price lever and see the demand impact in real time, using that retailer's confirmed elasticity. "What happens if we drop Kroger by 5%?" — answer in seconds.

🔍

Cannibalization Monitor

As new SKUs launch, Mo tracks whether growth is incremental or pulling from your existing line. The signal that FP&A needs before committing to a new SKU forecast.

The FP&A supercharger: Your analyst doesn't need to run a model. They ask Mo — "Is Kroger Brownie Batter growing from real demand or promotional dependency?" — and Mo pulls the live SPINS data, runs the decomposition, and returns a plain-English answer with supporting charts. The analyst's job shifts from building the analysis to acting on it.
